questions.question: Why does scaling writes require sharding rather than just adding replicas?
options: Replicas are too expensive
In single-leader replication every write still goes through one leader; sharding splits the data so each shard handles only its slice of writes
Replicas cannot store writes at all
Sharding is just another word for replication
questions.explanation: Replication copies the full dataset to every node, so all writes still funnel through the single leader — no write scaling. Sharding divides the data across machines so each shard absorbs only part of the writes (and stores only part of the data).

questions.question: What is the most important decision when sharding?
options: The programming language of the shards
The partition key — the attribute that decides which shard each row lands on
The number of CPUs per shard
The color of the dashboard
questions.explanation: The partition key determines load balance. A good key spreads load evenly; a bad one sends most traffic to one shard (a hot shard), giving you all the complexity of sharding with the throughput of a single machine.

questions.question: Sharding by 'country' when 80% of users are in one country causes a hot shard even with hashing. Why?
options: Hashing is broken for strings
The key has low cardinality — every row with the same country value hashes to the same shard, so the skew concentrates there
Country names are too long
Because hashing always creates hotspots
questions.explanation: Hashing scatters DISTINCT key values across shards, but identical key values always map to the same shard. With only a handful of distinct countries and one dominating, all those rows pile onto one shard. The fix is a high-cardinality key like user_id.

questions.question: What is the main downside of hash partitioning compared to range partitioning?
options: It cannot distribute load evenly
It scatters adjacent keys across shards, so range queries must hit every shard
It only works for numbers
It requires more disk space
questions.explanation: Hashing deliberately scrambles key order for even distribution, which destroys locality: keys that are adjacent in value land on different shards. So a range query ('keys M through O') becomes a cross-shard query touching every shard. Range partitioning keeps ranges on one shard but is prone to skew.

questions.question: Why does sharding by user_id help keep per-user queries efficient?
options: It makes user_ids shorter
All of a user's related data (e.g. their orders) co-locates on the same shard, so per-user queries and joins stay within one shard
It eliminates the need for a database
It guarantees strong consistency across shards
questions.explanation: Choosing user_id as the partition key co-locates everything belonging to a user on one shard. Per-user reads, joins, and transactions then stay within a single shard, avoiding slow cross-shard operations. Co-location is the key to keeping sharded systems fast.
